tools.name: query-blueprints
tools.description: Queries available blueprints.

tools.name: enable-blueprints
tools.description: Enables blueprints in the current conversation by their keys. Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled. Always call [query-blueprints] first to discover available blueprint keys.

keys.description: The keys of the blueprints to enable.

tools.name: refresh-blueprints
tools.description: Updates the blueprints saved to the database with the latest version from the registry. Sometimes blueprints in the database can become outdated. Use this function to pull in the latest versions.

tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: delete-object
tools.description: Deletes the object.

obj.description:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)

tools.name: update-object

obj.description:

- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [complete_job] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [complete_job] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [complete_job] with { "success": "undefined" }
Do not stop until you call [complete_job].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.
Once enabled, list the available mailboxes.
Then query the 5 most recent messages from the first mailbox found.

Completion criteria:
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled.
- Mailboxes are listed.
- 5 recent email messages are returned.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: enable-blueprints
tools.description: Enables blueprints in the current conversation by their keys. Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled. Always call [query-blueprints] first to discover available blueprint keys.

keys.description: The keys of the blueprints to enable.

tools.name: refresh-blueprints
tools.description: Updates the blueprints saved to the database with the latest version from the registry. Sometimes blueprints in the database can become outdated. Use this function to pull in the latest versions.

tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- ReadEmail (read-email) completes without error; the result reflects 0 emails (e.g. empty content).

Let me break down the task:

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel. Let me also query blueprints first to confirm availability.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

</output_format>

  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- ReadEmail (read-email) completes without error; the result reflects 0 emails (e.g. empty content).

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

# Summary formatting:

- Directly actionable.

- Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).

- [ ] Task description.

Additional information can be included (indented).

content.text: I can see the `org.dxos.type.mailbox` schema. It requires `feed` and `filters` fields. I need to first create a Feed object, then create the Mailbox referencing it. Let me also enable the inbox blueprint in parallel with creating the feed.
The feed was created and the inbox blueprint was enabled successfully. Now let me create the Mailbox object referencing the feed.

The Mailbox object has been created with DXN `dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCS`. Now I need to call the ReadEmail operation. The inbox blueprint is enabled, so the `read-email` tool should be available. However, I don't see it in my available tool list directly. Let me check if it's accessible - the tool name from the blueprint is `org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email`. Since I don't have a direct `read-email` function in my tool list, let me try querying the mailbox to see if there's a way to invoke it, or verify the mailbox exists first.

Let me verify the mailbox exists and check what tools I actually have available:

The Mailbox object exists in the database. Now I need to invoke the ReadEmail operation. The tool `org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email` was listed in the inbox blueprint. However, looking at my available function tools, I don't see a direct `read-email` function. The blueprint tools are operations that should have become available after enabling the blueprint. Let me check if there's a way to call it - it's possible these operations are exposed differently. Let me try loading the mailbox object to see if there's more context:

I see the mailbox exists. The inbox blueprint has been enabled and lists `org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email` as one of its tools, but this tool is not directly available in my current tool set. The tools I have access to are the database/blueprint management tools. The inbox operations (`read-email`, `classify-email`, etc.) are not exposed as callable functions in my environment.

Let me verify by checking the feed for any messages - if the feed is a queue, I can query it:

result.content:
The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- ReadEmail (read-email) completes without error; the result reflects 0 emails (e.g. empty content).

You manage my email inbox.

# Summary formatting:
- Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
- Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
- Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.

# References
- Use references to objects in the form of:
@dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
- References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.

# Tasks
At the end of the summary include tasks.
Extract only the tasks that are:
- Directly actionable.
- Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
- Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
- Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.

Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
- [ ] Task description.

Additional information can be included (indented).

Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.

content.text:
I can see the inbox blueprint is enabled with its tools. The `read-email` operation is listed as a tool in the blueprint, but it's not directly available as a callable function in my current tool set. The tools I have are limited to the database/blueprint management ones listed at the top.

However, the task says to "Call the ReadEmail operation" - let me see if the tool is now actually available to me after enabling the blueprint. The blueprint system may have dynamically added it. Since I can't see it in my static tool list, let me report what I've accomplished and the limitation.

Actually, wait - let me re-read the task. It says the tools from an enabled blueprint should appear. Let me check if there's a `read-email` function I can call. Looking at my available functions again, I don't see one named `read-email` or `org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email`. But the prompt says "NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear." 

Since the blueprint was successfully enabled, the read-email tool should now be available. But in my current function list, I only see the original set of tools. Let me try to complete what I can and report the results.

Let me try to add the mailbox to context to see if that triggers anything:

The mailbox is now in context. Unfortunately, the `read-email` tool (`org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email`) is not available as a callable function in my current tool set even after enabling the inbox blueprint. The blueprint was successfully enabled, but the inbox operation tools are not exposed as direct functions I can invoke.

Let me report the results:

success.readEmailResult: The read-email tool (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) is listed in the enabled inbox blueprint but is not available as a callable function in the current tool set. The blueprint was successfully enabled with zero rejections, and the mailbox exists in the database with an empty feed (0 emails). The operation could not be directly invoked because the inbox operation tools are not exposed as callable functions to the agent.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [complete_job] with the output.

If no output is required, call [complete_job] with { "success": "undefined" }
Do not stop until you call [complete_job].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- ReadEmail (read-email) is available and completes without error; the result reflects 0 emails (e.g. empty content).

Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [complete_job] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [complete_job] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [complete_job] with { "success": "undefined" }
Do not stop until you call [complete_job].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  - org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.database "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  - org.dxos.blueprint.inbox "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/memory "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  - dxos.org/blueprint/automation "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  - org.dxos.blueprint.web-search "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.

content.text:
Let me break down the tasks:

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.

content.name: create-object

content.text: The inbox blueprint is enabled. Now I need to check the schema for `org.dxos.type.mailbox` to understand the required `feed` field, then create the mailbox properly.

content.text: Now I can see the mailbox schema. It requires a `feed` field (a reference to an `org.dxos.type.feed` object) and a `filters` array. I need to first create a Feed object, then create the Mailbox with a reference to it.
Feed created. Now I'll create the Mailbox with a reference to this feed.
The Mailbox object has been created. Now let me call the ReadEmail operation using the mailbox DXN. The inbox blueprint is already enabled, so I should have access to the `read-email` tool.

content.name: read-email

The blueprint enablement returned a list of tools (`org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email`, etc.) but those tools are not actually surfaced in my available toolset. Let me try the exact tool name from the blueprint.

content.text:
The inbox blueprint was enabled successfully (confirmed by the enable-blueprints response), but the tools it declares are not available in my current tool list. This appears to be a system limitation where enabling a blueprint registers it but the tools don't dynamically appear for the agent in this session.

Let me verify the mailbox exists and report my findings.
The Mailbox object exists in the database. Now let me report the results:

failure.message: ReadEmail tool not available despite inbox blueprint being enabled
failure.description:
Successfully completed 2 of 3 criteria:
1. ✅ Mailbox object created in database (DXN: dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDA, name: 'Primary Mailbox', with associated feed).
2. ✅ Inbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) successfully enabled — confirmed by enable-blueprints response listing tools: read-email, classify-email, draft-email, summarize-mailbox, google-mail-sync.
3. ❌ Could not call read-email. After enabling the inbox blueprint, the declared tools (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email, etc.) did not appear in the available toolset. Tried both 'read-email' and 'org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email' — both returned: "Failed to find tool with name '...' in toolkit". Available tools remained: query-blueprints, enable-blueprints, refresh-blueprints, add-to-context, remove-from-context, load-object, create-object, delete-object, update-object, query, create-relation, delete-relation, add-schema, list-schemas, add-tag, remove-tag, complete_job.
